title: Co-optimization of spin coherence and valley splitting in Si/SiGe heterostructures
authors: Peihong Zhang, Xuedong Hu, Saif Ullah, Jason R. Petta
abstract: Single electron spins can be used to encode and process information in semiconductor quantum devices. Progress has been hindered by materials challenges, such as the small energy splitting between low-lying valley states and hyperfine coupling to nuclear spins. Here we use density functional theory to optimize the valley splitting and spin dephasing time in realistic Si/SiGe heterostructures. Reductions in the Si quantum well width generally increase the valley splitting. However, in narrow quantum wells, a larger fraction of the electronic wavefunction resides in the SiGe buffer layers, which increases the hyperfine coupling with spinful $^{73}$Ge. Our work shows that Si/SiGe heterostructures with 3~--~4~nm wide quantum wells and $^{73}$Ge and $^{29}$Si concentrations of 50 ppm should support average valley splittings $E_{v}$~$>$~500~$μ$eV and spin dephasing times $T_2^*$ exceeding 15~$μ$s assuming an effective quantum dot area of 700 nm$^2$. In addition, sharper Si/SiGe interfaces in general result in larger valley splittings and longer spin dephasing times.
summary: This theoretical study uses DFT to optimize Si/SiGe heterostructures for quantum computing by balancing two competing factors: valley splitting and spin coherence. The authors find that achieving high performance requires narrow quantum wells (3-4 nm) combined with precise isotopic purification to mitigate detrimental hyperfine coupling from nuclear spins.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of co-optimizing spin coherence time and valley splitting in Si/SiGe heterostructures for use in semiconductor quantum devices.
main_result: Optimal performance requires balancing the competing effects of quantum well width, suggesting that 3-4 nm wide wells with specific isotopic concentrations can yield both large valley splittings (>500 ueV) and long coherence times (>15 uS).
method: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were employed to model the electronic structure and calculate the hyperfine coupling responsible for spin dephasing.
model_or_system: The system is based on Si/SiGe heterostructures, specifically modeling the confinement of electron spins in Si quantum wells (QWs) sandwiched between SiGe barriers.
key_observables: Valley splitting (E_v), spin dephasing time (T_2*), and the percentage of electronic wavefunction in the barriers (p_B).
important_parameters: Quantum well width (t_QW), isotopic concentrations (e.g., 50 ppm of 73Ge and 29Si).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on DFT calculations, and the dephasing rate is calculated using the Fermi contact term approximation for hyperfine interaction.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how growth profiles and confinement lift valley degeneracy (Fig. 1), how wavefunction overlap with Ge nuclei increases in narrow wells (Fig. 2), and how interface quality affects T_2* (Fig. 3).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical challenges (small E_v, hyperfine coupling) and then uses DFT to model the system. It analyzes the trade-off between narrowing the QW (which increases E_v but also increases coupling) and proposes optimal structural and isotopic parameters to maximize both metrics.
why_interesting: This work directly tackles the material science bottlenecks for solid-state qubits, providing quantitative guidelines on how structural parameters (like interface sharpness) and material composition (isotopic purification) must be controlled to realize functional quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Growth profile of a typical Si/SiGe heterostructure. The silicon quantum well (QW) has a thickness tQW and is buried by a Si0.7Ge0.3 spacer layer of thickness ts. Spinful 29Si and 73Ge nu- clei result in electron spin decoherence through the contact hyperfine interaction. Inset: Atomic scale renderings of the Si and Si0.7Ge0.3 layers. (b) First Brillouin zone of bulk silicon with six equivalent val- leys along the high-symmetry directions in k-space. (c) The six-fold valley degeneracy is lifted by tensile strain and confinement along the growth direction (ˆz).

FIG. 2. (a) An ideal Si/Si0.7Ge0.3 heterostructure with atomically abrupt interfaces. (b) Charge density, ρ(z), of the two valley states (VS 1 and VS 2) in the Si QW as obtained from DFT calculations us- ing the heterostructure illustrated above. (c) Percentage of the elec- tronic wavefunction residing in the SiGe barrier layers, pB, as a func- tion of tQW. pB rapidly increases as tQW is decreased below ∼5 nm, exacerbating the effects of hyperfine coupling to spinful 73Ge nuclei.

FIG. 3. (a) Ev as a function of tQW for idealized structures with atomically abrupt interfaces. The solid red line depicts the average valley splitting ⟨Ev⟩. (b) Contributions from individual nuclear spins to the contact hyperfine coupling in a QW with tQW = 3.2 nm. The blue (orange) dots represent contributions from 29Si (73Ge) lattice nuclei.

FIG. 4. (a) Color-scale plot of the inhomogeneous spin dephasing time T ∗ 2 as a function of 29Si and 73Ge concentrations. (b–d) Linecuts extracted from panel (a). In panel (b), T ∗ 2 is plotted as a function of 73Ge concentration for 50 ppm 29Si. In panels (c,d), T ∗ 2 is plotted as a function of 29Si concentration for 800 ppm and 50 ppm 73Ge.

FIG. 5. (a) Ev for QWs bounded by 1 nm thick Si0.85Ge0.15 transi- tion layers. Results from four different SiGe structures are shown. The red solid line shows the average valley splitting ⟨Ev⟩for these structures. For comparison, the results from Fig. (3) are plotted here as well (blue line, abrupt interface). (b) A comparison of T ⋆ 2 as a function of tQW for abrupt interfaces (dashed lines) and broadened interfaces (solid lines).
